title: MiFID II Best Execution — Client Limit Order Price Improvement vs Top of Book
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the MiFID II programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with MiFID II control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.

compliance.assurance_rationale: MiFID II Art.27 — UC provides continuous Splunk telemetry evidence for this regulatory requirement, uplifted from contributing to partial after review confirmed the SPL captures relevant monitoring signals.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that MiFID II Art.27 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.5.23: MiFID II Best Execution — Client Limit Order Price Improvement vs Top of Book.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.5.23' running on index=trading sourcetype="ems:fill" (order_type, limit_px, best_bid, best_ask, exec_px, side, client_segment), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'MiFID II Best Execution — Client Limit Order Price Improveme' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper MiFID II control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the MiFID II control objective.

dataSources: `index=trading` `sourcetype="ems:fill"` (order_type, limit_px, best_bid, best_ask, exec_px, side, client_segment)
app: EMS and market data via HEC (Splunkbase)
spl:
index=trading sourcetype="ems:fill" order_type="limit" client_segment="retail" earliest=-30d
| eval improvement_bps=if(side="B",(best_ask-exec_px)/best_ask*10000,(exec_px-best_bid)/best_bid*10000)
| where improvement_bps < 0
| stats count as worse_than_tob, median(improvement_bps) as med_imp by venue
| sort - worse_than_tob
description: Limit orders should receive fair treatment relative to displayed liquidity. Measuring frequency and magnitude of price improvement versus top-of-book at execution time supports conduct narratives for retail protection.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for MiFID II compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes trading and sourcetypes ems:fill. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Validate `best_bid/ask` timestamps align with fill; (2) handle inverted books; (3) retail-only scope; (4) investigation workflow; (5) link to smart order router version. Indexes required: `index=trading`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=ems:fill`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **End-of-day batch reconciliation** — Automated nightly reconciliation processes between the order management system and the trade repository generate high-volume record comparison events that are a normal part of MiFID II best-execution reporting obligations.

2. **Market maker quoting activity** — Designated market makers operating under exchange agreements generate continuous quote streams and rapid order cancellations that are legitimate trading activity rather than market manipulation indicators.

3. **Corporate actions and settlement adjustments** — Position adjustments triggered by dividends, stock splits, mergers, or settlement instruction amendments create expected transaction pattern changes that do not indicate reporting failures.

4. **Regulatory reporting system maintenance** — Scheduled ARM/APA connectivity tests, reference data updates, and approved reporting system failovers produce transaction reporting gaps or anomalies that are operationally expected and pre-communicated to compliance.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `mifid_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved batch job identifiers, market maker firm codes, and scheduled maintenance windows. The search filters matches where `exception_type` is populated and within the valid exception time window.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `trading` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.27`. The control objective is: "Evidence that MiFID II Art.27 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.5.23: MiFID II Best Execution — Client Limit Order Price Improvement vs Top of Book." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `trading` under `ems:fill`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Validate `best_bid/ask` timestamps align with fill. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** handle inverted books. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** retail-only scope. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** investigation workflow. Routing targets (SOC, compliance team, specific queue) must be documented with named on-call owners; an 'alerts go to the team' runbook fails at 3am on Sunday.

• **Hint 5:** link to smart order router version. Routing targets (SOC, compliance team, specific queue) must be documented with named on-call owners; an 'alerts go to the team' runbook fails at 3am on Sunday.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

The detection fires when `improvement_bps < 0`. For "MiFID II Best Execution — Client Limit Order Price Improvement vs Top of Book," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the MiFID II control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time. This threshold is the operational heart of the control — set it too low and the signal drowns in noise; set it too high and real compliance deviations go undetected. The value `0` should never be treated as universal; it must be calibrated against your specific operational baseline.

Calibration procedure: before enabling this as a production alert, run the base search over the last 30 days with the threshold removed and compute distribution statistics:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats count by improvement_bps
| stats avg(improvement_bps) as mean, perc50(improvement_bps) as p50, perc90(improvement_bps) as p90, perc99(improvement_bps) as p99, max(improvement_bps) as hi
```

Set the production threshold between p90 and p99 depending on your organisation's alert fatigue tolerance. Document the chosen value in the control specification alongside the date of calibration and the sample size — auditors will ask how you arrived at `0` and the defensible answer is 'data, plus a documented review by the control owner,' not 'the template said so.'

The `median()` aggregation over the last 30 days is a deliberate choice. Compliance reports on sustained behaviour, not instantaneous spikes — a single noisy event crossing the threshold for one second should not page the on-call. If your organisation requires point-in-time sensitivity (e.g. real-time fraud detection), shorten the time window and aggregation span; for accountability reporting, widen them. Tune for the regulation's actual expectation, not engineering preference.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.5.23: MiFID II Best Execution — Client Limit Order Price Improveme]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -30d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.5.23,reg=MiFID_II
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=trading sourcetype=ems:fill earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `improvement_bps`, `med_imp`, `venue`, `worse_than_tob`. Run:

```spl
index=trading sourcetype=ems:fill earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table improvement_bps, med_imp, venue, worse_than_tob
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `improvement_bps`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(improvement_bps) as p50 perc90(improvement_bps) as p90 perc99(improvement_bps) as p99 max(improvement_bps) as hi
```

The current threshold `0` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.5.23: MiFID II evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Histogram (`improvement_bps`):** Reveals the distribution shape of the measured metric. Useful for calibrating thresholds — the shape tells you whether the current threshold sits on the tail or in the body of the distribution.

**Panel 2 — Drill-through table (`venues`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 3 — Single-value KPI (`worse fills`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'MiFID II current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Too many or too few alerts:** The threshold is almost certainly miscalibrated for your estate — re-run the percentile calibration (Step 3d) on a representative 30-day window and pick a value between p90 and p99.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**MiFID II-specific failure modes:**

• Best execution evidence (RTS 27/28): MiFID II requires venue-level best execution reporting. Detections must cover ALL execution venues the firm uses, not just the primary — a detection missing a single venue fails the 'all venues' requirement.

• Transaction reporting (MiFIR Art. 26): T+1 reporting to competent authority. The detection's latency must be tight enough to catch missing-report within the T+1 window; a 7-day cadence is regulatorily too slow.

• Pre-trade and post-trade transparency: pre-trade quotes and post-trade trade reports are separate obligations. Detections on transparency must differentiate; missing pre-trade quote != missing post-trade report.

• Systematic Internaliser thresholds: SI designation triggers at specific volume thresholds. Detections on SI obligations must read the latest SI thresholds (ESMA updates them periodically); a hardcoded threshold goes stale within months.
grandmaExplanation: We limit orders should receive fair treatment relative to displayed liquidity.
